Text: I agree with people who say your work is phenomenal. Then send out a regular mailing about your latest work, lessons and workshops you're offering (you can make good money on workshops..most artists make $3000 for a weekend workshop) use Mailchimp, it's free...squarespace has a free website template where you can sell things... you got this! Text:
I have some thoughts for You that I wish I learned way earlier in my own career. 

Think more in terms of business. And depersonalize your art. If it works. Do more, if it doesn't. Do less. Make small bets, small projects that aren't the most time consuming. Bc time is your most important  resource.

It's important to find your niche. Seems like you have it, now you need to find who wants your work. 

I heard this on a business podcast - why do robbers rob banks? Because it's where all the money is. 

Go to where your audience is. And share the work. If they like it. The market will respond. If they don't. Adjust and try again. The example they gave was a girl that painted horses really well like really really good, and couldn't sell anything. Zero sales. 

A mentor told them to set up an easel and paint horses near this specific area. It was a trail head or something that horse riders frequent. People saw the paintings, and wanted to buy paintings of their horses. And she got a ton of commissions. 

Because A. People that own horses - have money, because horses are expensive. 
And B. they also want nice things especially if they relate to their expensive hobby. Surprise surprise. 

Find your audience.

I would suggest everyone here who's struggling with their art to watch this keynote speech by Seth Godin -  [Your Job is to make Art. ](https://m.youtube.com/watch?v=rdUeq09cGJ0) If you're paying attention, I think it will be some of the most rewarding 45 minutes you've spent on the Internet.

Text:
I'm going to be a bit harsh here. You undervalue yourself, & you let your clients undervalue you, as well, and you want to quit, because of it. I'm sorry, but that just sounds so silly. Your work is incredible! You understand that you're undercharging your work, but to offer discounts, free work and to continue being so accommodating. It sounds like you're a very kind, understanding person and your heart is in the right place. 

Unfortunately, business can't always be so kind, as you are. A lot of people drop pleasantries with money and sometimes you'll end up with people who will take advantage of things like being able to lie about a shipment not making it to them for refunds. 

I'm sorry that you've attracted clients that don't value your work or you as a person. I personally would recommend drawing a boundary with your business. Stay firm on your decisions, draw crisp lines between what is acceptable business for you. If there's a reason for charging less (wanting to be affordable to people poorer), than fine, but *boundaries*! If it's a self confidence issue that you think you can't charge more, I think you have a bunch of artists already in this thread chiming in that you have the talent and you should charge more! Consider expanding where you do business, charging more for your work, and reevaluating boundaries. 

If you say "the customer pays for shipping" so be it. If you say, "Pay me in my preferred currency", so be it. You set the rules for your work, not the clients. They're not the ones running your business. It's okay to be a little more selfish and to be more confident that your pieces will sell at a higher price.

Text:
When selling art you have to look for signs that tell you if you're setting the prices well or not. If you get a good amount of traction but all they do is complain about the prices, and you find yourself not having the issue resolved by lowering the price, then you are usually taking the wrong action. Usually people who don't draw don't know how much work it goes into this craft, those will always conplain but people who have commissioned artists usually know it better and they just find someone in their price range. 

Set the price higher. Compare what people with similar skill to you charge and adjust the pricing accordingly.

Then you have to keep in mind this: If you get too many people interested, that's a sign that you should up the pricing. If your customer number is too low, you set it lower. But first and foremost, don't let your customers tell you what you should do; they are customers. The really bad ones will try to get what they want by any means, this includes lying to you or undervaluing your work because you will bend under the pressure and they get whay they want to have that way. Look at what other artists do, they have a similar experience and if they mean well they will give you good tips.

But that's not how art commissions work, you set a price and they have to deal with it because, well, art comissions aren't a necessity but a luxury to afford as you comission people who are qualified - that have a skill and give you a custom piece of art. Commissioned/custom pieces are supposed to be expensive, look at prices of custom cosplays, furniture and so on, the price will ALWAYS be much higher because the making is very time consuming and it requires skill. It's not a machine made copy, it's unique.

Text:
Ah man. You’re doing soft pastel too- those materials are $$$$

Time to try another tactic to make people want to buy: explain your medium and why it is expensive (pure pigment, no oils, no yellowing, high archival life). Gives a perspective to the buyer that what they’re purchasing is equal to or superior to classic oil paintings. Often a challenge to overcome because oil is held in high regard.

Get yourself out there into real world markets. If you haven’t got a website, I suggest doing so. Your art is professional-level and deserves to be shown off and featured in a curated manner. Business cards- hand them out like they’re candy.

Text:
I also do  pet portraits, and I’m sorry your clients so far have been so crappy, but if you’re only finding them on Reddit they might just be young. People are nuts about their pets, and they will pay WAY more than you are charging! I think you need to find local clients. Make a nice flyer for your pet portraits, and ask local vet offices if you can put it up. In exchange, offer to do a free portrait of the vet’s own pet.

To figure out your prices, when you do your next portrait, log how many hours it takes you. Pay yourself a living wage commensurate with your education, experience, and quality of work (please consider at least $30/hour). Then add the full cost of materials, shipping, conversion, tax, etc. The total is your new price. 

Standardize pricing based on size and subject matter (ie 11x17” with one dog is x price, adding an additional pet is y price, 18x24” is z price, etc).

But seriously, try to find local clients, or set up a nice Etsy shop and advertise it on Instagram (be sure to build the Etsy fees into your prices). It sounds like you’re dealing with dummies online who have no idea what art is worth, and you’re selling yourself short. People with money will pay for good art.

Text:

Tangentially, as someone who has local prices and USD prices for international clients, a fear I have is for enterprising clients to try and pretend they're local to avail the cheaper prices (it has not happened so far but I have seen stories of people receiving such clients).

Difference in economies can really affect pricing, like USD has very high purchasing power so a lot of artists from my country can race to the bottom with their pricing. But, on the flipside, if I actually did a 1 to 1 conversion of my international pricing to my local ones, then local clients can not afford my art and I want to keep things accessible to them specifically.

Text:
In my opinion your art, monetarily speaking, is worth what people want to pay for it.  If your setting the price before an offer is made, start with how much you want to get paid per hour, then calculate the number of hours. You take that and add the cost of you to replace the supplies when you need to. But remember to prorate based on the amount of the material you use including the cost of matting and framing. For example, I work with colored pencils and use many different colors at a time, but I do not use the entire pencil for one drawing so I will not add $2 per pencil I use I will just add some to help restock when that time comes. Remember to think about the tax you have to pay and your overhead depending on if you are full or part-time with your art.

When you take the $100 if you spent more than 10 hours (including the rough sketching, planning and prep time)you are making less than $10 dollars per hour. Now subtract your expenses; what are you left with? Did you get what it was worth?

Text:
This is what I do. I couldn't stand the possibility of losing out on so many hours of work, and having to keep images I didn't even want. I picked it up after a few high school blunders of using my finest paper and pens only for the person to say "oh that's not at all what I asked for" . Aahhh...

I'm sure someone's gone over it but I've been safe with using estimation for the initial cost. I have an idea of how many hours I spend in certain tasks, and create a range of minimum - maximum hours. Then I charge half of the minimum as security deposits before I start any work. I am also very forward that "it's possible to go beyond the maximum estimated hours possibly due to requested changes".

Once I receive the payment, I'll do the work and keep a time card. I keep in touch during the process with low resolution watermarked samples. Once it's done I send a final low resolution image to confirm it's what they want, and send the final invoice for the remaining hours before sending the full glory image. Both invoices act as a terms and conditions (contracts) but the final one is to say that we've both agreed it's complete, and won't do further work on it once payment is received. 

It's kept me safe, but I've also been very lucky to have amazing clients so far.

Text:
Okay, while I agree that artists should charge way more than $20, there are a few conditions to actually raise your price. First and foremost, when you start monetising your artwork, you become a business. That means you gotta know your product, your audience, and the value that you can bring to that audience. Are your works good enough/valuable? Note that sometimes it's not only about skill/technique. If you can make something that relates to your audience a lot, they'll buy it. Or at least, they'll share it so it might reach people that will buy it.   


You also got to know your audience. early 20s or below usually doesn't have hundreds of $ to spend on art. So it's either you shift to mature audience that can actually afford it(but that means you gotta level up your work a lot to compete with other professional artists), or you think of a way/strategy that still affordable yet able to give you profit. Other people says prints(or any small merch for that matter), and that's good, especially if you have a fanbase.  Tiktok art video maybe? Youtube?  


Last thing. This might be because I'm growing old and grumpy. Why y'all young artists rush to sell your artwork just after one or two people says "oh I like your work, you should sell it"? That will just add so much unnecessary pressure when you should focus on studying and improving your artwork. If you need money, take part time jobs. When your works improved and become valuable, people will notice you. Then you can take some commissions and raise your price whenever you got overbooked
Label: analysis

Text:
step one : find a way to mass product your art for cheap, so you can still sell each product for $20-30 a pop. my original ink pieces are priced around $60 at the moment but they were originally prices at $20 when i had no buyers.

step two: do actual promo events for your art. gallery showings, convention tables, interact on social media, gather fellow artists around you, social events with drinks and food....basically socialize & talk to people! **as an artist, you need to input love in order to receive love.** . 

step three: when you get an increase in audience and start selling more. Even better, invest your time in larger scale pieces when you do have an audience you can sell to, for higher prices. 


Be patient and pace yourself too! it's important to note that growth that leads to sales, takes a long time to get better overall.

 it is totally okay to approach your work in a casual way, especially if you still have a day job or other full time responsibilities. The thing that matters most is if you enjoy it. if you are showing that you enjoy the process of your growth, people will gravitate to you naturally.

Text:
I'd be happy to help! I started out by decking out alot of my own stuff. I'd make stickers, buttons, post my art to facebook, etc. I never made prints, but I guess making custom buttons was my start? Mass producing work always leaves extra unless it's made to order. I'm very green so didn't care for that. I'm a jack of all trades so if someone mentioned "oh bro that'd be so funny haha I'd love X" I'd look them dead in the eye and say "I can make that. You actually interested?". I got a decent number of commissions that way. I started under priced like everyone else.
If you want to regularly do commission, find your market. I know, easier said than done, but you're basically running a small business. Make business cards, get social media, if you can make decor for your own spaces do it. If I go out I weak my button saying open for commissions, and some other artwork. I pass out cards like candy. I also can read people pretty well. Being observant helps you find your market. If I talk to older people, I show them my charcoal drawings of animals/scenery. If it's people our age, I show them fanart or characters or dumb meme art.

My main source of commission are nerds. I'm wired into Dungeons and Dragons (big money), Magic the Gathering (also can be good), and a few other nerdy hobbies like Gaming. I make stuff for myself like custom art Magic cards and play with those to lure people in. I post on fan groups on twitter or reddit. Facebook is where you get age 30+ commissions frequently.

So my pricing is in USD, so you might need to do a little conversion but ~
If it's just a one medium, one color drawing (like ink and paper) it can range from 20$ -120$.
Charcoals usually are 60$
Custom art Magic cards range from 20- 100$ depending on what all I'm changing.
I made a custom Pip Boy from Fallout, that was a trade but I did the math and it would've been 250$ since it took so long. 

If you want my art accounts you can DM me, or if you've got more questions I'm happy to answer!

Text:
Fair enough. Yeah selling your stuff for a fair price is a difficult task at hand. 

I was offered an already problematic job (realizing someone else's design) this month and after their first offer I got literally and openly laughed at when I stated what I'd consider a fair price. First they started at 20% of what I had in mind and when I shared it then they told me that the maximum is 30% of that. So then I declined. On the other hand, on a meeting last week I stated my price and the reaction was "um, ok, maybe we'll stretch the project through the next year then" ... 

Point is, some people are willing or able to pay well/fairly, some aren't. Your job as an artist, like it or not, is to find the people who are. It's very annoying because that's not what we signed up for, but realizing this is what turns you from an amateur into a professional. And being actually able to do it turns you from being a starving artist into a successful artist.

So, for your own mental health, try to focus as little as possible on people who don't have the budget to pay you, they are wasted time and energy, and instead try to reach people who are worth your time. That second story's client I met directly through a previous client who I worked for, for 200% of the money I said would be fair to the person in the first story.
